findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: a workplace incident on March 14, 2021
findings.description: The brief states the workplace incident occurred on March 14, 2021. The medical records, police report, and witness statement all state the incident occurred on March 12, 2021, which conflicts with March 14.

evidence.quote: a workplace incident on March 14, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021

I, Diane Tran, provide the following statement of my own free will regarding the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief says March 14, while three separate source documents expressly say March 12. This is a direct, verbatim date conflict.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE or fall-arrest equipment. The police report and witness statement state the opposite: he was wearing a hard hat and harness, and his lanyard detached only because the anchor point was part of the collapsed structure.

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.

Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse. The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way. Ellison stated that the anchor point was part of the section that collapsed.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief says Rivera lacked required PPE, while both police and witness evidence expressly state he wore a hard hat and harness. The lanyard evidence also directly rebuts any implication that he lacked fall-arrest equipment.

findings.title: Time-barred heading contradicted by timely filing under SOL
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations (CCP 335.1 / two years = 730 days). Filing on 2023-03-10 is 726 days after the incident on 2021-03-14, which is inside the 730-day window. The time-barred framing is therefore self-defeating.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief's own body text concedes the filing falls nominally within the two-year window, which directly undercuts the categorical 'time-barred' heading. Confidence is slightly reduced because the sentence reserves an accrual-date argument, making the framing misleading rather than purely self-contradictory.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief's own body text concedes the filing falls nominally within the two-year window, which directly undercuts the categorical 'time-barred' heading. Confidence is slightly reduced because the sentence reserves an accrual-date argument, making the framing misleading rather than purely self-contradictory.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: Privette is a real, well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted sentence is materially overbroad. The word “never” is not faithful to the doctrine: Privette bars a particular theory of vicarious liability to contractor employees, not all liability by a hirer in every circumstance arising from contracted work. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that the peculiar-risk doctrine does not allow an independent contractor’s employee, covered by workers’ compensation, to recover tort damages from the person who hired the contractor merely because the work was inherently dangerous. California’s Privette doctrine is qualified and later cases recognize exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution and concealed hazards.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted 'never liable' language is materially inconsistent with the well-known qualified Privette doctrine and its exceptions. Confidence is not higher because the evidence does not include the actual Privette passage for verbatim comparison.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaf
findings.description: The record supports that Apex employed Rivera, but the claim that Apex, not Harmon, was responsible for scaffolding operations is undercut by evidence that Harmon's foreman directed the crew to work on the section and dismissed a specific safety concern about the base plate. The words "not Harmon" conflict with evidence of Harmon's direct involvement in work direction and safety-related decision-making.

evidence.quote: Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.

Tran further stated that she and other crew members had raised concerns about the condition of the east-side scaffolding earlier in the week, noting that some of the cross-braces "didn't look right" and that the base on the far end "was sitting on a piece of plywood that kept shifting." Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The source quotes directly show Harmon personnel directing the work and dismissing a scaffolding safety concern, which undercuts the brief's categorical 'not Harmon' framing. Some uncertainty remains because employment responsibility and operational/safety responsibility can be legally distinct.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated proposition: Privette v. Superior Court
findings.description: The quoted proposition that a hirer is “never liable” is an overbroad and likely inaccurate characterization of Privette. California's Privette doctrine is a presumption/nonliability rule with recognized exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution, negligent provision of equipment, and concealed hazards. Privette does not establish a categorical rule of no hirer liability in every injury arising from contracted work.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702.

findings.confidence_reasoning: This is supported by the canonical Privette doctrine and its recognized exceptions, but the evidence supplied for this finding is only the brief's short citation rather than the original Privette text. It duplicates the more complete quote-focused Privette finding.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: I do not recognize this Ninth Circuit case, and the quoted rule is suspicious: a broad “OSHA compliance creates a rebuttable presumption of due care” rule is not a familiar California negligence rule. The case name is generic, the proposition fits the brief too neatly, and 887 F.2d is more consistent with late-1980s reporting than a 1991 Ninth Circuit decision, adding a citation-integrity concern. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort-law standards except as persuasive authority.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The reporter/year mismatch is a concrete citation-integrity signal, and the asserted OSHA-presumption rule is not a familiar California tort rule. Confidence is below mechanical certainty because no external database result is provided.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.
findings.description: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case. Concrete red flags include the highly fact-matching party name and subject matter—“Delgado Scaffolding Co.” in a scaffolding-collapse brief—and a parenthetical that fits the brief's argument unusually neatly, including an assumption-of-risk gloss that is not a standard Privette formulation. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a federal district court decision applying California law would be persuasive at most and not binding on a California Superior Court.
findings.brief_location: Section III.A (end of first section; “See also”).

evidence.quote: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The finding rests mainly on nonrecognition and contextual red flags, not a database lookup or conflicting reporter record. It is kept because the party name and proposition are unusually tailored to the brief, but the citation is not conclusively disproved from the supplied evidence.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023 â€” one year and 362 days
findings.description: The supplied documents do not verify the complaint filing date, but they do contradict the incident date used for the elapsed-time assertion. From the source-supported incident date of March 12, 2021 to March 10, 2023 is 728 days, i.e., one year and 363 days; even using the brief's March 14, 2021 date, March 10, 2023 is 726 days later, i.e., one year and 361 days, not one year and 362 days.

evidence.quote: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023 â€” one year and 362 days after the incident.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The date conflict is explicit, and the day-count arithmetic is mechanically checkable: March 12, 2021 to March 10, 2023 is 728 days, not the brief's stated elapsed time. Confidence is slightly reduced because the filing date itself is not independently verified by the provided documents.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
findings.description: The source file includes a witness statement taken by plaintiff's counsel describing unsafe scaffolding conditions and Rivera's compliance with PPE protocols. That is incompatible with the broad assertion that Rivera presented no evidence rebutting safety compliance.

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
I hold a competent person certification for scaffold erection and inspection under Cal/OSHA standards.
Before we started, I walked the section to do a visual check. I noticed several things that concerned me:
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing. The plywood had shifted noticeably since I had last seen it a few days earlier.
Carlos was doing his job and following safety protocols. The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The witness statement gives specific safety-condition evidence that could rebut the brief's safety-compliance framing. Confidence is below mechanical certainty because 'presented' could refer to the litigation record before the court, not merely documents in the verification file.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated proposition: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
findings.description: Seabright supports the Privette delegation doctrine, especially as to Cal-OSHA duties, but I do not know it to hold that statutory-safety compliance is “highly probative” of due care. The brief also overstates the effect of compliance by saying it “effectively insulat[es]” Harmon from tort liability; Seabright is about delegation of duties, not an evidentiary presumption from OSHA compliance.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

findings.confidence_reasoning: The distinction between Seabright's delegation holding and an evidentiary presumption from OSHA compliance is a strong legal-characterization point. Confidence is limited because the actual Seabright passage is not quoted in the evidence.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Harmon's unblemished OSHA inspection record demonstrates that its safety practic
findings.description: Although the file does not contain OSHA inspection records, the sentence also asserts that Harmon's safety practices met or exceeded all regulatory requirements. The witness statement directly undercuts that framing by describing improper footing, worn components, dismissed safety concerns, and an unsafe scaffolding section.

evidence.quote: Harmon's unblemished OSHA inspection record demonstrates that its safety practices met or exceeded all regulatory requirements
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing. The plywood had shifted noticeably since I had last seen it a few days earlier.
Two of the coupling pins on the second tier looked bent or worn.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The witness statement materially undercuts the broad claim that safety practices met or exceeded all requirements. Confidence is moderated because no OSHA inspection records are provided, so the 'unblemished record' part is unsupported rather than directly contradicted.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Rivera filed the instant action on March 10, 2023.
findings.description: No complaint, docket, proof of filing, or other court filing document is included in the sources. The provided medical, police, and witness records do not establish the filing date.

evidence.quote: Rivera filed the instant action on March 10, 2023.

findings.confidence_reasoning: A filing date normally requires a complaint, docket, or similar court record, and the identified source set consists of medical, police, and witness materials. Confidence is limited because the absence of all litigation records is asserted rather than shown by quoted source text.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: a licensed subcontractor retained by Harmon for scaffolding assembly and related
findings.description: The source documents support that Apex was Rivera's employer and performed scaffolding work, but they do not state that Apex was licensed or that Harmon retained Apex. Those specific assertions are not established by the provided record.

evidence.quote: a licensed subcontractor retained by Harmon for scaffolding assembly and related work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted brief language contains specific assertions about licensure and retention, and the upstream description says the provided record lacks those facts. Confidence is limited because the evidence excerpt here does not show the full universe of source documents or their silence.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
findings.description: The brief repeats a March 14, 2021 incident date. The sources consistently place the incident on March 12, 2021, including the medical intake, police report, and witness account.

evidence.quote: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
On the morning of March 12, 2021, our crew arrived at the site at approximately 7:00 AM.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief's March 14 date directly conflicts with medical, police, and witness records stating March 12. This is the same underlying wrong-incident-date defect preserved in finding-14.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the
findings.description: The scaffolding-collapse portion is corroborated, but the date is not. All source documents identify March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021, as the date of the incident.

evidence.quote: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021

At approximately 10:45 AM, I was working on a scaffolding section adjacent to where Carlos was positioned, roughly 20 feet to his west. I heard a loud metallic snap â€” it sounded like a brace or coupling failing â€” followed immediately by the sound of the structure collapsing.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The date portion of the brief quote says March 14, while medical and police records say March 12; the witness statement corroborates the collapse circumstances but not the March 14 date. This is the same underlying wrong-date defect as finding-14, repeated in the fact section.

could_not_verify.title: Could not verify quotation attributed to Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
could_not_verify.description: I do not genuinely know Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. or its language. The citation is suspicious—the Federal Reporter volume/year appears off for a 1991 Ninth Circuit decision—and the asserted OSHA-compliance presumption is not a general OSHA rule I recognize; OSHA compliance is usually evidence, not an automatic presumption of due care. But without knowing the case or confirming the citation, I cannot honestly classify the quote as accurate, doctored, or fabricated.

evidence.quote: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

could_not_verify.title: Outside the case file's coverage: Harmon maintained an active Injury and Illness Prevention Program ("IIPP") and h
could_not_verify.description: The provided documents do not include Harmon's IIPP, OSHA inspection records, or any February 26, 2021 inspection record. This concerns internal safety-program and inspection-history materials outside the coverage of the supplied file.

evidence.quote: Harmon maintained an active Injury and Illness Prevention Program ("IIPP") and had passed all OSHA inspections conducted at the site during the relevant period, the most recent being February 26, 2021.

could_not_verify.title: Outside the case file's coverage: Harmon's documented compliance with all applicable OSHA regulations during the r

could_not_verify.title: Outside the case file's coverage: The risks associated with working at height on scaffolding are inherent to his t
could_not_verify.description: Whether the risks were "well known to him" concerns Rivera's subjective knowledge and acceptance of risk. The supplied documents do not verify Rivera's internal state of mind.

evidence.quote: The risks associated with working at height on scaffolding are inherent to his trade and were well known to him at the time of the incident.

could_not_verify.title: Outside the case file's coverage: As a trained professional, Rivera understood the hazards of scaffolding work and
could_not_verify.description: The assertion that Rivera understood and accepted hazards is subjective state-of-mind and assumption-of-risk boilerplate. The provided documents do not establish his subjective understanding or acceptance.

evidence.quote: As a trained professional, Rivera understood the hazards of scaffolding work and accepted them as part of his employment.

judicial_memo: Overall, the brief contains several serious factual and authority-related defects that warrant close scrutiny. Most concretely, the Introduction states that the workplace incident occurred on March 14, 2021, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all identify March 12, 2021; Section II further states Rivera was not wearing required PPE or fall-arrest equipment, but the police report and witness statement say he wore a hard hat and harness and that the lanyard detached because the anchor point was part of the collapsed structure. The brief’s “time-barred” heading is also inconsistent with its own limitations calculation, which places a March 10, 2023 filing within a 730-day period even using the brief’s March 14 date. The Privette quotation appears materially overbroad in using “never,” contrary to the qualified doctrine and recognized exceptions. In addition, eight cited authorities could not be located or were not recognized in available review, with varying confidence.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), is a well-known California Supreme Court decision; the reporter, volume, page, and year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that an employee of an independent contractor generally may not recover from the hirer under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries covered by workers' compensation. It is the foundation of the California rule that a hirer presumptively delegates workplace-safety responsibility to the independent contractor.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition is a fair modern statement of the Privette doctrine: hirers are generally/presumptively not liable for injuries to the contractor's employees arising from the contracted work. The brief should not present the rule as absolute, however; California recognizes exceptions, including retained control affirmatively contributing to injury (Hooker), negligent furnishing of unsafe equipment (McKown), and concealed hazardous conditions known to the hirer (Kinsman).

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: The citation is an id. citation to Privette, a recognized California Supreme Court case. I cannot verify the exact wording at page 702 from memory, but the underlying authority exists.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette generally bars an independent contractor's employee from suing the hirer under the peculiar-risk doctrine for work-related injuries covered by workers' compensation.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted proposition that a hirer is “never liable” is an overbroad and likely inaccurate characterization of Privette. California's Privette doctrine is a presumption/nonliability rule with recognized exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution, negligent provision of equipment, and concealed hazards. Privette does not establish a categorical rule of no hirer liability in every injury arising from contracted work.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case. Concrete red flags include the highly fact-matching party name and subject matter—“Delgado Scaffolding Co.” in a scaffolding-collapse brief—and a parenthetical that fits the brief's argument unusually neatly, including an assumption-of-risk gloss that is not a standard Privette formulation.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify that the case exists, I cannot assess whether it supports the asserted proposition.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a federal district court decision applying California law would be persuasive at most and not binding on a California Superior Court.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this Ninth Circuit case, and the quoted rule is suspicious: a broad “OSHA compliance creates a rebuttable presumption of due care” rule is not a familiar California negligence rule. The case name is generic, the proposition fits the brief too neatly, and 887 F.2d is more consistent with late-1980s reporting than a 1991 Ninth Circuit decision, adding a citation-integrity concern.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the case, I cannot evaluate support. As a substantive matter, the asserted OSHA-compliance presumption is suspect; compliance with safety regulations may be evidence of care, but it generally does not automatically insulate a defendant or create a broad presumption defeating negligence.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort-law standards except as persuasive authority.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), is a well-known California Supreme Court Privette-line case; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that a hirer presumptively delegates to an independent contractor the hirer's tort-law duties to provide a safe workplace, including duties imposed by Cal-OSHA regulations, and therefore the hirer generally is not liable to the contractor's employee for breach of those delegated duties.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright supports the Privette delegation doctrine, especially as to Cal-OSHA duties, but I do not know it to hold that statutory-safety compliance is “highly probative” of due care. The brief also overstates the effect of compliance by saying it “effectively insulat[es]” Harmon from tort liability; Seabright is about delegation of duties, not an evidentiary presumption from OSHA compliance.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a familiar California statute establishing the limitations period for personal-injury actions.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of, an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The statute supports the asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year limitations period. However, the brief's broader time-bar argument is undermined by its own dates: an injury on March 14, 2021 and filing on March 10, 2023 appears to be within two years, absent some unusual accrual or tolling issue.
